items.myth: groupby and merge are pure data-wrangling steps with no effect on model validity.
items.reality: Aggregations and joins decide what information each row carries; global group stats and label merges are among the most common leakage sources in tabular ML.
items.example: A user-level mean computed on all data before split makes validation look strong but fails on cold-start users in production.

items.myth: left join and inner join are interchangeable if you fill NaN afterward.
items.reality: Inner join permanently removes unmatched left rows from the dataset; filling NaN cannot recover rows that were never merged.
items.example: Inner join orders to customers drops guest checkout rows entirely—left join keeps them with NaN customer fields for a missingness feature.

items.myth: transform and agg are interchangeable because both use groupby.
items.reality: agg collapses to one row per group (entity-level features); transform preserves row count (row-level normalization). Swapping them changes model grain and join keys.
items.example: Using agg mean when you need per-event demeaning produces a tiny summary table that cannot merge one-to-one back to event rows without another join.
